cards.text: Food Web: The inter-connected food chains operating in an ecosystem which establish a network of relationship between various species, are called a food web.
Transfer of Energy
Trophic Levels - The various steps in a food chain at which the transfer of food takes place are called trophic levels. In a food chain, each step representing an organism forms a trophic level.
The sequence of living organisms in a community in which one organism consumes other organisms to transfer food energy is called a food chain. The simple food chain operating in grass land is:

Functions of Ecosystem:

  • Ecosystem keeps its component parts running together by maintaining the interactions stand exchange of materials between biotic and abiotic components.
  • Another important function of ecosystem is biological diversity and maintenance of stability.

Decomposers
Most of the microorganisms, such as bacteria and fungi, cannot synthesis their own food and feed on dead parts of the plants and animals, waste products and animal excreta. Such type of organisms are called as decomposers.

Detritivores: Some consumers which feed on dead remains of plants and animals (detritus) and breakdown it into smaller fragments are called detritivores. Detritivores are also known as scavengers. Examples of detritivores are earthworms, termites and vultures.
Parasites: Some consumers which live on or inside the body of other organisms to obtain their food are called parasites. Example of parasites are tape worm, liver fluke and round worm.
A generalised scheme of nutritional relationship among different biotic components of an ecosystem.

Carnivores
The animals which feed on the flesh of other animals are called carnivores. Examples of carnivorous animals are lion, tiger, wolf, snake, frog, vulture, kingfisher, lizard and hawk, etc.

Consumers
All those organisms which are unable to make their own food are called consumers or heterotrophs. They take ready-made food from the plants either directly or indirectly for their survival. All animals come in this category.

Biotic Components:
All the living beings make up the biotic components of an ecosystem. It includes plants, animals and microorganisms. On the basis of mode of obtaining food, living beings present in an ecosystem are divided into three groups— Producers, Consumers and Decomposers.

Abiotic Components:
All the physio-chemical factors of an ecosystem are called as abiotic components of the ecosystem. These components are divided into three categories —

  1. Inorganic substances
  2. Organic compounds
  3. Climatic factors

Artificial Ecosystems: Those ecosystems which are maintained artificially by man are called artificial ecosystems. The examples of artificial ecosystems are croplands such as maize, wheat, rice fields, orchards, gardens and aquarium, etc.

Natural Ecosystems: Those ecosystems which operate themselves under natural conditions without the major interference by the man are called natural ecosystems. Examples of natural ecosystem are a pond, a forest, a grassland, a river, a sea, etc.

Ecosystem: An ecosystem is a self-contained unit of living things (plants, animals and decomposers), and their non-living environment (soil, air and water). For example; a forest, a pond, a lake, a green land etc.

Biodegradable Wastes: Substances that are broken down by the biological processes are said to be biodegradable. These substances are decomposed through the actions of fungi, bacteria, and other living organisms. For Examples: Food waste, trees leaves, urine and fecal matter, sewage agricultural residue, paper, wood, cloth, cow-dung etc.

Non-Biodegradable Wastes: Substances that are not broken down by biological processes. For Examples: These includes DDT (Di-chloro-di phenyl trichloro ethane-in-pheneyle the cheoro ethane), insecticides, pesticides, mercury, lead, arsenic aluminum, plastics, polythene bags, glass, radioactive wastes.

Harmful effects of biodegradable and Non-Biodegradable Substance

  1. The waste destroys the natural beauty and our surroundings become dirty.
  2. Decomposition of these wastes results in the production of a foul smell, which spreads to the surrounding areas.
  3. These wastes may also block the drains creating pools of waste, which become the breeding sites of mosquitoes. The latter is carriers of diseases like malaria and dengue.

Natural ecosystems are further classified into terrestrial ecosystems and aquatic ecosystems.

  • Terrestrial ecosystems are located on the land area.
  • Grasslands, forests and deserts are the examples of terrestrial ecosystems.
  • Aquatic ecosystems are located in water area. They are of two types: marine ecosystems and fresh water ecosystems.

Producers
The organisms which can make their food from simple inorganic substances present in the environment are called producers. Producers are also called as autotrophs. Green plants and a few bacteria are the example of autotrophs.

Herbivores
The animals which feed on the plants and their products are called herbivores. Cow, rabbit, monkey, goat, elephant, zebra, giraffe, squirrel, sheep, ass, ox, buffalo, horse and hippopotamus, etc. are some of examples of herbivores.

Omnivores
Those animals which feed on plants, as well as animal products, are called omnivores. Examples of omnivores are man, bear, cockroach, crow, sparrow, dog, ant, etc.

Functions performed by the decomposers

  • They clean the nature by decomposing dead parts of the plants and animals, waste products and animal excreta.
  • They help in the recycling of the nutrients in the nature so that a balance is maintained in the nature.
  • They replenish various minerals and nutrients in the soil as a result of which fertility of the soil increases.

Significance of Food Chains

  • The study of food chains helps in understanding food relationships and interactions among the various organisms in an ecosystem. The food chains, transfer energy and materials between various living components of an ecosystem.
  • The food chains transfer energy and materials between various living components in an ecosystem or biosphere.
  • The food chains give dynamicity to an ecosystem or biosphere.
  • The movement of toxic substances like pesticides, weedicides, etc., through food chains, can prove very harmful.

Ten Percent Law: According to ten per cent law, only 10 percent of the energy entering a particular trophic level of organisms is available for transfer to the next higher trophic level.
Example - Suppose 1000 Joules of light energy emitted by the sun falls on the plants.

flash_cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text: Composting: Organic wastes are filled into a compost pit (2m {tex}\times{/tex} 1m {tex}\times{/tex} 1m). It is then covered with a thin layer of soil. After about three months the same garbage filled inside the pit changes into organic manure.
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text: Recycling: The solid wastes is broken down into its constituent simpler materials. These materials are then used to make new items. Even non-bio degradable solid wastes like plastic, metal can be recycled.
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text:
Waste Management
The waste generated in our houses is called garbage. The household waste is in either solid or semisolid form and generally excludes industrial hazardous wastes.
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text:
Formation of ozone molecules:

  1. The high energy UV radiations break down the O2 molecules into free oxygen (O) atoms.  
    O {tex}\xrightarrow{{UV}}{/tex} O + O (atoms)
  2. These oxygen atoms then combine with oxygen (O2) molecule to form the ozone molecule.
    O2 + O {tex}\to{/tex} O3 (ozone)
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text: Depletion of Ozone Layer: Ozone (O3) layer is largely found in the stratosphere which is a part of our atmosphere from 12 km -50 km above sea level. This region is called the ozonosphere. Ozone is deadly poisonous at the ground level.
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text:
Effects of ozone depletion
The depletion of ozone allows harmful ultraviolet radiations to enter in the atmosphere of the earth. Ultraviolet radiation is highly damaging to organisms as it causes skin cancer in human beings, damage eye and immune system. Due to damage in the immune system, the body loses its resistance power and thus cannot fight against diseases and germs. Ultraviolet radiations also affects the plant life. In plants, these radiations reduce the rate of photosynthesis by 10 to 25 per cent.
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text:
International Efforts to check Ozone Depletion
Recognising the harmful affects of ozone depletion, an international agreement, known as Montreal Protocol was signed at montreal, Canada in 1987 to control the emission of ozone depleting substances. Afterwards, many more efforts have been made and protocols have laid down definite road maps, separately for developed and developing countries, for reducing the emission of CFCs and other ozone depleting chemicals.
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text: Biodegradable Wastes: Those substances which are broken down into simpler, harmless substances in nature with due course of time by the biological processes such as action of microorganisms are called as biodegradable wastes. Domestic waste products, urine and fecal matter, sewage, agricultural residue, paper, wood, cloth, cotton, wool, jute, cattle dung, tea leaves and vegetable peels are examples of biodegradable wastes.
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text: Non-biodegradable Wastes: Those substances which cannot be broken down into simpler, harmless substances in nature with due course of time by the biological processes such as action of microorganisms are called as non-biodegradable wastes. DDT, plastics, polythene bags, insecticides, pesticides, mercury, lead, arsenic, aluminium, radioactive wastes etc. are the examples of non-biodegradable wastes.
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text:
Waste Management  mainly includes:

  • Food and kitchen waste as vegetable and fruit peels.
  • Paper, glass, bottles, cans, metals and plastic.
  • Waste clothing, waste plastics such as toys, etc.
  • Some domestic hazardous wastes are medication, electronic waste, paints, chemicals, light bulbs, fluorescent tubes, batteries, shoe polish etc.
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text: Landfills: A landfill is a low-lying, open area usually far away from the living places of people where solid waste materials are dumped. After filling the waste, landfill is covered with the soil. This covered landfill is left for 25 years and more.
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text: Incineration: Burning of the waste at high temperatures (usually more than 1000oC) to form ash is called as incineration. It is carried out in incinerator. This method is used to dispose the household waste, chemical waste and biological waste of the hospitals.
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text: Sewage Treatment: The waste water released from different sources is called sewage. Sewage is a liquid waste which contains harmful substances and disease-causing microbes. So, before it is disposed into a water body, it is first treated to remove pollutants. The treatment of sewage is done in the sewage treatment plant.
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?

cards.text:
Eco-friendly Practices in Our Daily Life

  • Switch off electric appliances when unnecessary.
  • Close the engine of your vehicle on traffic lights.
  • Use public transport instead of own car.
  • Use water in mug instead of running tap water while shaving, etc.
  • Use eco-friendly cloth or jute bags instead of plastic bag.
  • Use energy efficient CFL lights instead of normal bulbs. Energy efficient CFL lights use one quarter of energy then the normal bulbs.
cards.topic_name: How do our Activities Affect the Environment?
